one_line_takeaway: A privacy-compliance gateway for enterprise LLM use that fine-tunes a small (3B) domain model to detect sensitive entities and format-preservingly encrypts them before prompts leave the org, with RFT-based reasoning giving the best generalization to unseen policies while SFT gives the best raw detection/anonymization.
key_finding: On the in-taxonomy test set, DLMS variants dramatically outperform the untuned Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct base and the paper's stated Llama-Guard-3-8B baseline. DLMS-SFT is best at safety detection (Accuracy 0.935, F1 0.918, AUPRC 0.962) and at entity anonymization (Privacy Hiding Rate 0.839) versus base model's 0.468 accuracy and 0.064 PHR. DLMS-RFT-CL is best at the category level (Subset Accuracy 0.698, Multi-Label F1 0.735, AUPRC 0.742, Hamming 0.928). RFT models underperform SFT on entity extraction (PHR 0.555 RFT, 0.533 RFT-CL) because conservative overprediction penalties added to curb reward hacking made them overly cautious. The headline generalization result: on unseen non-taxonomy policies (few-shot), the RFT-CL stage-1 model reaches Accuracy 0.862 / F1 0.861 / AUPRC 0.898, beating the SFT-LoRA (0.758/0.749/0.832), SFT full-FT (0.564/0.455/0.769), and base (0.713/0.706/0.801) models -- supporting the claim that the 'analyze-then-decide' RFT paradigm generalizes to evolving policies without retraining. FPE preserves format so Copilot returns near-identical responses on encrypted vs plaintext prompts.
core_contribution: LLM Access Shield is an end-to-end framework that sits between organizational users and external LLM services (Copilot, ChatGPT, Perplexity) to enforce privacy-policy compliance. Its own contributions are threefold: (1) DLMS (Domain-specific LLM for Security), a Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct model fine-tuned to detect sensitive data in user prompts, trained two ways -- Llama-Guard-style supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and DeepSeek-R1-Zero-style reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) using an 'analyze-then-decide' reasoning paradigm with <analyze>/<answer> tags; (2) dynamic, real-time policy customization that generalizes to unseen (non-taxonomy) privacy policies via few-shot prompting without retraining; (3) a Sensitive Data Anonymizer (SDA) that applies Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE, NIST FF3-1) to detected sensitive entities so structure/format is preserved and the prompt stays usable by the downstream LLM, with decryption of entities in the returned response. The authors also investigate three-stage curriculum learning (CL) for RFT and reward-hacking mitigations. Note: cryptographic methods (HE, MPC, TEE, DP) and prior sanitizers (EmojiCrypt, ProSan, Casper, ConfusionPrompt, PP-TS, HaS, DePrompt) are cited baselines/related work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Organizational users interacting with untrusted third-party (public) LLM services may inadvertently or deliberately leak sensitive data (PII such as email/phone/ID/bank account, plus monetary/financial/confidential org data) into prompts sent to external servers. Public LLM services are modeled as honest-but-curious: they follow protocol but may infer or retain sensitive info from inputs. The integrator, DLMS, and SDA are trusted and run inside the organization's secure boundary; a MITM proxy with an installed CA certificate intercepts/forwards traffic; the FPE algorithm is assumed secure against known cryptanalysis per NIST. The paper cites Chu et al. (prompt-based reconstruction of private conversation data from GPT) as motivating attack and notes ~35% of security incidents stem from deliberate/accidental employee actions.
limitations: (1) Curriculum learning induces catastrophic forgetting: the CL stage-1-only checkpoint scores highest on safety (Acc 0.965) but stages 2 and 3 degrade safety performance (down to 0.916) as later tasks interfere with earlier ones. (2) RFT's conservative reward design lowers recall / Privacy Hiding Rate, a self-identified reward-imbalance problem. (3) The taxonomy is small (only 6 categories) and evaluation datasets are modest (2,311 train / 1,542 test in-taxonomy; only four non-taxonomy policies). (4) An internal inconsistency: the text (Sec 7.1) says DLMS beats the 'baseline Llama-Guard-3-8B' but Table 5's baseline row is Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, so no 8B Llama-Guard numbers actually appear. (5) The framework only protects user prompts; preventing leakage of privileged internal knowledge through LLM responses is left to future work. (6) FPE security rests on assumption, not new cryptanalysis; utility of FPE is shown only qualitatively (one Copilot example, no quantitative downstream-task metric). (7) Non-taxonomy test prompts are LLM-generated, and no public benchmark exists, so external validity is limited.

datasets_used: Custom in-taxonomy sensitive-data dataset: 2,311 training samples (28.99% safe, 71.01% unsafe) and 1,542 testing samples (29.31% safe, 70.69% unsafe), 60/40 split, over 6 categories T1-T6 (email, personal ID, phone, fax, bank account, monetary value)

Non-taxonomy evaluation set: four authentic privacy policies from an anonymous org's Data Protection Office; unsafe prompts generated by seven uncensored 8B-13B HuggingFace LLMs, safe data drawn from four public human-LLM interaction datasets
quantitative_results: DLMS-SFT safety level: Accuracy 0.935, F1 0.918, AUPRC 0.962 (best safety detector)
DLMS-SFT entity level Privacy Hiding Rate 0.839 (best); DLMS-RFT 0.555; DLMS-RFT-CL 0.533; base Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct 0.064
DLMS-RFT-CL category level: Subset Accuracy 0.698, Hamming Accuracy 0.928, Multi-Label F1 0.735, AUPRC 0.742 (best category)
Base Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct safety Accuracy 0.468, F1 0.462, AUPRC 0.869
Non-taxonomy few-shot: DLMS-RFT-CL (stage-1) Accuracy 0.862 / F1 0.861 / AUPRC 0.898, beating DLMS-SFT-LoRA (0.758/0.749/0.832), DLMS-SFT-FPFT (0.564/0.455/0.769), and base (0.713/0.706/0.801)
CL forgetting: stage-1-only safety Accuracy 0.965 -> stages 1&2 0.951 -> full RFT-CL 0.916
RFT reward components: format +2/-2, safety +1/-1, category +2/+1/-1, entity +4/+2/-1
baselines_compared: Llama-Guard-3-8B (referenced in text as baseline, though no numbers in Table 5)
DLMS-SFT vs DLMS-RFT vs DLMS-RFT-CL (internal training-method comparison)
DLMS-SFT full-parameter fine-tuning vs DLMS-SFT LoRA (non-taxonomy generalization)
FPE (FF3-1) vs AES for anonymization ciphertext format preservation
Qualitative/related-work comparison table vs EmojiCrypt, ProSan, Casper, ConfusionPrompt
